speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This interview with Nellie Wood Smith took place at her Bovill home on November 13th, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I know them, know them well. In fact, they were running that paper when the piano contest was on, the popularity contest. I was entered in the contest when a friend of ours, well, I was boarding with us, so I was entered for competing in that contest. So, there was, well, I don't know how many of them, I think there was about twelve or more that were entered in the contest. And, so, and besides, I had been canvassing for an Eastern firm. I had, well, jewelry and kitchen utensils and silverware and all those certain things that I was canvassing for them and I'd deliver on the soft cashier. So, I was pretty well trained in  at that time, too. I serviced, or canvassed the town. Of course, it wasn't much of a job, it wasn't so big as all that, but I know. That was 1910. But, I can't think of that other, Carl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Carl Peterson?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Sam and Carl and Homer, the three brothers. And, Sam married my great uncle, my mother's uncle. And, her name was then, her name was Angie Peterson, and she married Uncle Robert Smith, Angie Smith. She was buried up here at the Deary Cemetery. He died before she did, though, over in Oregon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was this popularity contest?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, we had to get subscriptions for the Deary Enterprise and they would get so many points for each subscription. And then, when their paper came to them, there's a coupon in each paper. If you kept that coupon, there was another hundred or two, or whatever it was. Well, I walked, I think it was twelve miles, when I'd make a trip up into the woods. The lady lived across the, we lived right around that corner over there when I was a girl. And, that lady lived just across the road from me. Well, her husband was working in the woods, and the kids in school, and she took it on herself to go with me, you know, see me. You know, I didn't like to go in places like that alone, out in the camps. But, she went with me on my trips, and we walked as much as twelve miles a day, making the rounds of those trips to collect those coupons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And, sell subscriptions?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh huh. Sell subscriptions and stuff. And also, there was coupons, trading coupons that you could buy with the dry goods store here. They were in on it, Grant and Giles, dry goods. And, you sold them a coupon for maybe ten dollars. I mean, it was a ten dollar coupon, but by selling them that coupon, they traded out, they got it for about seven and a half, or something like that. And, they got the value of it in the material.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was at the same bar, the same deal?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, and that is part of it, is this paper, and the subscriptions, and the coupons that were in the paper, and then the coupons, the cash coupons from the store down here, the dry goods store. And, I guess that was all that was concerned. And, it was the Derry newspaper. Yes, they were part of it. And, they were all combined into that get up, you know, then they were all, whatever you got from those people, was part of your coupon, your account.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You actually went around and sold to the Lumberjacks?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes. They all knew me. I remember they knew my dad, you know. You know, I told them who, I told them, explain what this is. Now, if there's anything they need to buy at the dry goods store, like if they handled all the men's fittings, you know, they worked clothes, and they didn't have, most of them didn't buy too many fancy clothes. They were busy working. They bought their shoes and their clothing, and they had a color of jewelry too. They could sell, they bought the coupons for the jewelry or their clothes, either one in the store, they're the same store. And, so, and you could sell that. Well, I had several jewelry coupons, and I sold several. And, anyhow, when the finals came, they was, I had about a million and a half points. And, it closed the next, the second one, it was about a million. So, I'm on the piano, and it sits over there in the corner now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You won a piano?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, yes, that's what the store thought. The winner would win a piano. And, it was sitting there in the store downtown, and you could all see what you were getting, see what you were looking at. So, that's how I got my piano, and I wouldn't have had one if it hadn't been for that, because, I know my dad was just working at Carpentries, working here and there, and it took about all that they had. And, sometimes, Mother would take in washing when he was out of a job. You know, some of her friends that just didn't, had youngsters, big family, and they'd have had Mother do part of the washing or something like that, or do some sewing. She, Mother, was quite a seamstress. And, this, well, Mrs. Anderson was one of them. One of the first ones we got acquainted with when we first came here. Her husband was Barbara. But, she just couldn't sew for sour apples, because she'd get her, you know, she'd try awfully hard, but she just didn't know how, and she'd find it, wind up with about, seam about that thick, you know, and then sew through it, and, you know, look about you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, she asked your mother to do the sewing for her?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She asked her, and her mother did, she asked her mother to do sewing for her, and both her, oh, something for herself, and mostly for the kids. You know, she tried hard enough, but she just didn't say anything. So, that was the story of the piano.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were young, like, as a young person, with the family not making very much money, did you find there were things that you wanted that you couldn't have, that you liked?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, yes, there were plenty of things, but I was satisfied. I knew they couldn't be had. I thought, satisfied is what I got. Go ahead and make something that takes its place, or something like that. I was never all tied up and lamenting about something I couldn't have. I knew the folks couldn't, but I didn't want it. If they had to build out a pitch, you know, to get it, I wasn't interested in it. So, that didn't bother me much, because I thought, though, I was staying within our means line, and then I was always, I mean to brag, but I was always about the best dressed kid in the school. Both in Troy, we always schooled out at Troy, it's out there where the Spring Valley Road turns. My schoolhouse, I say mine, was right in that little three -cornered place, before that little barn sits and the road turns to the end of the highway. There's a little triangle there, that's where our schoolhouse sat. It was called the Free Coinage, and the kids nicknamed it the Three -Cornered Schoolhouse, instead of Free Coinage.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was it called Free Coinage?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know, it was something that went back further than my time. I wonder if it had to do with the free silver or all that they talked about, they wanted money to be cheap. I don't know about that, but that was the name of it from the beginning. But that came there in 1903.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you say you were about the best dressed kid there?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I was, not to mean to brag, and nothing that we did without, but the mother was, well, there was eight children there in her family, and there was four boys and four girls. The girls all, they weren't in the word elaborate at all, but they bought nice things, and they could wear them forever, and they never wore them out, or couldn't tell, until they'd get so tired of them, and finally they just rolled up a big bundle and sent it to Mother. Here, make the kids something out of these if you can. And that's how I had such good clothes, because we couldn't afford to buy the material that they had bought for their dresses. And so that's how I had such nice clothes. When Mother was an expert, you might say, at sewing.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So, that's the story of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She would remake, she'd take the dresses and make new ones from you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, she'd rip them up and make me clothes that fit me, and tailored, and looked as nice as, just arrived from Paris, I guess, just arrived from the store somewhere.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that most of the kids' or the kids' families put much emphasis on looking good in appearance at the schools in those days? Was that very important to many families?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It was just ordinary. It was just that I had nice materials that I didn't buy, and the folks didn't buy, they'd all give them to me. And somebody had a Mrs. Lisell Mudge, and he met her. And I want to say her mother was Mrs. Miller, and they lived on the homestead. I mean, we did. We lived on their little house there, probably built a little door place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you lived at their house?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, they had a little house off from there, I guess. We got that, I don't know how it came that they had that place built. I think they kind of batched there themselves, fixed their own home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that was near your place?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It was right up in the orchard, just a little, maybe as much as a block from Miller's house. Three quarters of a mile from there was our place. We built our house, but we had to build a road up the hill and turn, and a few turns in it. It was kind of a long mile, but strictly I think it was about three quarters of a mile.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you were starting to say about Lisell Mudge?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: my dad had met her father. He was a barber in Troy. And they have on those Troy Days, you know, celebrations, they have pictures of the old -timers there, and her father is on that picture. They had a very big, and I've seen it in the windows several times, I've been down there, on the celebration days. And the other fellow, he had an extra barber that worked for him. I could have told you his name, but I wasn't trying to. But anyway, a young fellow. And dad had met them. He met Mr. Miller and liked him, and they talked a lot. Dad invited him, but said, no, he hadn't been there for a very little while. He said that his wife wasn't acquainted with anybody. They were just kind of strangers there. Dad said, well, he said, well, come on out to our place on Sunday. We had no big way of getting in there. No transportation of any kind. And I said, come on and bring the wife out and spend the day for a break for her. It would be doer good. So he said, well, you just do that Sunday stays. So on Sunday, out to Kay, Mrs. Miller was expecting a, she had one little girl about, oh, maybe two years old, something like that. And she, that was Mrs. Love, Newzell's sister, was there first time. And she was expecting Newzell at that time. Most any time. It's almost time for her. So that's when I got acquainted with Millers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this have to do with sewing somehow?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I couldn't say that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or the clothes that people wore?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes. Oh, I could say there's one skirt that Mrs. Miller gave Mother. But she saw what she did. They got acquainted and were real good friends. And she, I guess, saw some of the things that Mother had made for me. And then Edna was just three and a half years younger, so that's part of what she could use for her. Anything that you couldn't make up for me would probably fit in. You'd get things out that would fit her. And I just want to say that I think I've got a picture on here somewhere with the skirt on, of Mrs. Miller. Gave Mother that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you living near Troy at that time?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We lived right there by the schoolhouse. Right, when the Millers came and visited. That's where the Dinsmoor Mill was. The Dinsmoor Mill was just up the road, or block maybe, from that turn there. And, uh, the, well, Miller was named after my uncle. That is my aunt's, my great aunt. The Dinsmoor Mill. The Dinsmores were relatives of ours. And when Homer, the Dinsmoor, their son, built the first garage, or service station. That service station there that stands just across from the bank. He put that in and ran it for years. And he was an un -legged man. He had the misfortune of, well, he got hit with a baseball and playing on the kneecap. And it started trouble. And then again, he was going with Florence McGarry, who, who set it up right close to where we were. And she was teaching school around different areas. At that time she was a wonderful teacher, too. And, uh,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: say, you know about pictures of any of those, many of those people, right?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: A lot of them I have. But I was going to say it made me think of her. I have her picture, but she was teaching, you know, some of the photos. And her husband. Well, you say that he got hit with a baseball and his legs started getting bad. Yeah, and then he would, she was teaching then up to Helmer or someplace up in that area. And he was still living out there at the mill, you see, with his folks. And he was about 21, I think, or 19, or 20 something like that. And he used to drive up there with his horse and his team and buggy. And on the way back, coming back there from her place, he started, he had to get out and open the gate there at the Dry Creek Mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where that is?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, that's where my other cousins were living there. They were running the other mill, Dry Creek Mill. And so they, he got out and then he went back on the buggy and got his toe on that little round toe, that little round step. And it slipped off and that hit him on the knee again. And from then on he had trouble with it. And it got so, it got hurt so bad he just found he'd be living on it. And he was active, was a monkey. And he would,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: he would, he was sitting on the front porch or something like that. And they'd all get into a romp, you know, playing and chasing each other around or something. And wanted to do some dirty trick for, you know, to play some prank on the other one. And then they'd get it. The other one would take it after him. You know, that's always, there was a whole bunch of us young kids there. I was there visiting at that time. And so when he, someone I remember, he, when he'd, he'd sit and walk down the steps. He'd just leap over, you know, and go on. That was his style. I'd get to the fence and maybe jump over him. And he, I know I was there one day when he jumped off the porch, oh it was I guess it was any higher than this. He jumped off onto the grass and started to go on somewhere. And at that time, he just curled up in pain and agony and just hurt so bad that he wouldn't play with Maggie. He wanted him to go to the doctor who was living around him. Nope, he wasn't going to go to any doctor. So.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, he just wasn't going to like the doctors. He didn't want to be petted or doctored at all. Like lots of men, I know. Most of them have to be ready to dive in there before you can get them to a doctor. And, God got one of them in there. But he's getting over that now. He's glad to see the doctor occasionally. So, then. He went to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then he finally lost it, huh?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He found it a good time to go to the doctor then after that. He was having a real lot of pain at most all the time. He went up there. He was just too worried. Gular Losis had set in under the kneecap.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so, they decided they had to be amputated. And soon, Dr. Crithers was running a hospital in Moscow there before Christmas time. And, he was in the hospital and they decided to check the leg. They said, that would have to come off. Homer Bult said, they were going to take his leg off. If he was going to die, he'd die all in one piece. He was going to have that leg taken off. He just couldn't imagine himself running around with one leg. You know, he didn't know much about it. So, they were going to take it off. But, that's up to there.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And, they operated. And, it happened. Well, at first they tried to scoop out the putrid material, you know, that had formed under there. And, they said you just could rake it out with your fingers. And, they tried that first, but when they opened up the bandages to inspect it, it had gone wild. And, they didn't take it off, but they came to investigate. It was taken off, I think four inches from the hip. Four or six, sorry, from the hip. But, they had an awful time. All of his relatives and everybody coaxed him and pleaded with him to have that removed. And, he was determined, he didn't want to. But, finally, this girl is going with her. And, his head set, and that had ended him in Florence. Because, he didn't want to. He wasn't going to be there. They were engaged. And, he wasn't going to be having a hobbling around. Oh, a hobby. Hippity Hop or something like that. He thought they'd be coming. Or, it was kind of a nickname, something they used to use.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hop Along?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, yeah, something like that. He was afraid that there would come some hippie or something. They'd always hop, he'd go up. Hippity Hop. Yeah. So, but they found out, and then she coaxed him too. He had said he wouldn't have anything further to do with Florence after that. He didn't want to. He figured he'd be disgracing her, and she was a fine girl. And, she was a smart girl. And, he didn't want her tied to an old, hoby hop, you know, like heaps going to be. And, she just begged and pleaded with him to have that. Maybe that would turn out on him. And, there's lots of people running around in the Smutty Lake that are quite important to him. She finally convinced him. And so, then they finally did, he decided then to let the go -ahead not break. And, they took that leg off. But, she was sincere about it now. So, finally they were, after he got well, and kind of get over his shyness about that leg. And, the trouble he had, that leg was, I don't know, it was in the summertime. I think, but anyhow, that was removed. And, his family ordered him the best artificial that they could locate anywhere. They took almost several hundred dollars that they had to give for that leg.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And, then he worked there for a while. And, they walked around with them at home there. And, they looked like they were going to use their hands on the home anyway. And, witty as it could be. So, they finally decided on Sunday he was going to go on up to the Dry Peak Mill. And, see, Con and Jenny.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Con and Jenny, they call them. They live there in Moscow, were Moscow residents. They live right there on 3rd and, what's that street, 2nd from Lade.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Adams?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, not Adams. I think it begins with A though. Well, it doesn't matter. Well, anyway, that's where they lived in Moscow. They did them in later. But, at that time, they lived up at the Dry Peak Mill. And, he would go up to see them. He had to go up and show off. He was getting along so good. He'd walk in on that leg and it just looked like he wouldn't know the thing that really mattered with him. So, he rode up. He was quite a horseback rider. For him, it was a tweeze. It was fussy. And, so he straddled his horse and started out for the Dry Peak. And, he rode up there in the back. But, on his trip up there, his stump, the leg, had, while they were riding with that saddle, it caused a rub. Oh, there's someone. Just a minute.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want to turn off your machine?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Hi, now. Here's a tool I borrowed from Marvin. I'll bring it back.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Okay. Thank you. Okay, John. Thanks a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean? Wayne? Huh? You mean Wayne?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Marvin. Now, Wayne. Yeah, I had Marvin. Uh -huh. I thought you meant Wayne. Yeah, I know. Okay. Thanks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, he was riding along and his stump was rubbing?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh -huh. He didn't realize it and all, but I think Roger Blister who did, you know, injured, he agitated that thing, some way or other. And, he would never put that leg on again. That ended that career. And, he went around with one crutch, had him reinforce crutch. And, later on, he got to be a heavy man. But, he already was dead from that time on. He was only 21 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he use a peg leg at all or just a crutch? One crutch?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He used a crutch and he would get around faster on that crutch than you and I could on three of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened with him and Florence?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, it wasn't so very long until they were married and they were happy as you please, you know. And, he finally got over his shyness, you know, and finally realized that he could be done and he made the best of it. And, they finally had, she went on teaching and they had a baby boy.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He now is in the Livid Water Power Company in Spokane. Fine, fine farm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, she loved him and it didn't matter to her?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, it didn't matter to her because he was the same homer that she always had been in. And, so he, that's the way their story goes, but what was I... It must have been really hard for him to adjust. I can imagine it must have been tough. It was because he was one of these proud fellows. He was always spic and span and proud of the peacock. And, he just couldn't see himself hobbling around on my leg. But, he finally got over that and after he built that, after they were married and I guess when their baby was small probably,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: they built that service station right at the bottom of the way. I guess the first was in Troy and it was quite a thing at that time. They were all so proud of that. I saw a big write up on the paper one time about that first service station in Troy. I think I have the paper somewhere.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And, he kept on with that one crutch. He had it reinforced until there was a big stop. That big at the bottom and it was all fixed to suit his case and he'd gotten kind of heavy at that time. After he was even long, he got quite a heavy man. He must have weighed, oh, 165 or 75 maybe. Even the Tuanahag. So, whenever he'd stop at the service station and someone would drive in, he'd go, Hi, there, bro. You know, he'd come out of highland and laughing and always greeted them. And, Holly took clean the windshield and just tell them a story. And, the paper said somebody, I don't think anybody ever drove away from his station that wasn't laughing. At the time, he was just kind of a clown anyway. He was just full of pepper and ginger. And, I just followed tricks and stories and things to make the people laugh.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: One of the things that you mentioned when we were talking before that I was thinking about was, you said that when you first came to Bovill here, that there were some girls, when you were going to school with the guy, that there was some jealousy. And, I was wondering what they were jealous of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told me they weren't that sharp and the teacher sort of favored them, but why were they jealous?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I guess they were average, but they were petted. She made pet him. She just took a fancy turn and she kind of made pets of them. But, she thought they were so very brilliant, so very smart, you know. But, they were just average, good, I guess good average kids as far as books concerned. But, their manners were rather poor. And, well,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I began to go to the parties and the young fella that lived here in town, lived with his sister there, and he took a fancy to me some way or other and paid more attention to me than she liked him, the older girl. And, so she was always throwing up something to me and throwing him up to me, you know. Well, I didn't have any strings on him. I thought he was nice and pretty and nice. Oh, there's another break.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Hi.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, my gosh, have you taken up residence here?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Last time I was here, you were here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How are you? Fine, and you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Can't see, I'm frosting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How are you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, fine. She threw it up to you, but...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know, I just went ahead pretty much with my own business. I had my studies to keep going, you know, and I decided I didn't like to quarrel with her or have any words with her, but she moved her seat and sat behind the two of us. She and her sister were the same as me and my sister, about the ages, if I can compare. And, they moved their seat then, so they were sitting right behind Edna and I. And, Elsie and Althea were there. And, so, and then they'd sit there and tickle him, and they'd whisper, whisper, if you want to teach me to play. And, you couldn't study to save your neck, because all the neck get as loud as it could, and they'd call a whisper, and they'd teach you to just let them talk.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And, then, every once in a while, they'd bring in his name, Harry's name. And, about a while, she'd tell something about me. I think I was going after Perry, or Perry was running after me, or that she was going to be put on time or something, I don't know, just anything, sweeping, something along. But, you know, I wasn't interested, because, well, we were good friends, but we were just everyday acquaintances. But, then it got so that afterwards, he would come by and pick me up and the nights we'd go to the whole, when you go to party, you took the whole family. They'd just, and we'd all get in one somebody's house, and each one would come and bring their family. Big, little old and young. They had babies, so they took them in the bedroom, stuck them in the bed, and let them go to sleep. You know, and they played games, and had music, and in my place, they had the piano, at that time, and Dad played the violin, I played guitar, and my sister played the mandolin, so we had music. And then, some of the boys, they played the harp. And, we just had a grand old time, lots of old time fiddling contest. We'd have old time pieces, and all those that they played.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of difference, was there much difference now between the get togethers in Bovill here, with the way they had them in the country, on the homestead? Was there much of a difference between the kind of entertainment?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, nothing at the homestead. I thought you said before how the fellows used to come in, and they'd stay all... They did, that is, but we didn't have any parties, nothing like that, but they would just come in, you know, we knew they were coming, probably. Maybe they would be back next Saturday, or maybe they wouldn't be. They just... It was more just visiting than a party. Yes. Well, in Bovill here, these parties, you talked to me about them before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people get together for, would it be, would they go way into the night, that kind of thing?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, they'd been, they'd been probably maybe 11, 12 o 'clock maybe. That's about all. They'd break up, and some of them might be working, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they all people who were close friends? How did the group form that they would get together?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, they just got acquainted with all their neighbors, and so they got to be a habit, and wherever they'd be next. They'd come to my house next Saturday or something like that, and that's about all that was to it, and everybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was all the neighbors, everybody?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, everybody wanted to come, they were just as many more, and it was much more fun. And they played all those silly games, you know, that they used to play so much. Spin the pan, spin the bottle, they'd come, and they'd even play host office and all those things. Did much competition go on among the girls for the young men, or was there such a... No, there didn't seem to be much, except this one. They seemed to be kind of a prize or something. And this girl wanted them, and she was, oh, I don't know, she was just, wasn't it particularly...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what is about to say?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know. She wasn't one that was particularly appealing to people. She had kind of a rude way with her sometimes, and kind of a haughty way, but if she could just... She kind of had bad boys of the night. And I didn't... Well, I'll come back to all of them, just to give the party, you know, just all the friends and all. She didn't come to that party so hardly ever.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were young women supposed to be quite reserved?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There weren't rules like that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, they were all kind of like home folks. We were all home folks together.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I know that... And this one fellow, he was just sociable, like you walk down the street with him, you know, and that's the way we went together. And he was just a wonderful person. Anyhow, and he lived just right, just about the second door from us. And his sister and her two young children.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he kind of get it? Okay, the medicine down, is that good?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Mm. So, I went to... I don't know how long he'd be fine, so he'd come after me and they'd be going maybe next door, maybe two or three doors in the way, or whatever. It was just mostly that route and that end of town was mostly what I took in. So, it was the Hazes and the Rogers and the...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Smiths and... I don't know, I don't know if you remember this. One of my girlfriend's schools was Florence Smith. She was Mrs. George Wiley. That's another one you would see. And she'd have to be down at Derry. And she used to live in Derry. And her husband was George Wiley. He was... I believe the post -master there in Derry, for violin. And he was an awfully nice kid. Real... Hell. Little thing. He was just a good, straight, honest guy. And one day, over we were all standing around the street in recess, early morning recess. That was when we were in ninth grade. And there was Florence and George and myself and Otto Krashe and I don't remember who all else were there. But we were standing around there, I think they had a room, talking. Some of them had left their lunches or something. And one of them was one of these tin boxes of wafers. These sugary kind of wafers. And George said, let's have a... Somebody said, let's sample those and let's have a lunch. They were all laughing at each other. And we ate those wafers. Most of them, anyhow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then we said, well, now that's kind of sparrows, see? How are we going to get out of that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And he said, well, we don't want you going to pay for that. No, he said, no, he won't. The time you're doing this. And so he paid for the wafers. Told the teacher that he'd eaten those wafers. And offered to pay for them. I don't think the teacher even took any pay. But we figured maybe he'd left that and got that for his lunch. But I'll never forget that time. Really all the kids had to eat those wafers. That's when we were starved at all. That's when it was says.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it his lunch box?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I think it was in his lunch box. Probably didn't bring anything but that. I think he was going to eat it for his lunch. We always figured he was anyway. And after he got eaten, I thought, he's got to do something about that. We wanted to all go and tell him. We'd fess up if we wanted to tell him that we had eaten them. And George said, no, I'll go. I'll tell him. So I said, alright, the teacher just laughed and got a big joke. That was the kind of guys I jumped around with.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: You know, you talk about the dances and that kind of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the people who were on bug roll? The people who were the well -to -do?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: That was up at the other end of town. Beyond up where the house, the living house up there is, you know, that was the hospital. After it was built there. That built for a hospital. But anyhow, the people, T .P. Jones, the wood superintendent, he was. And, well, the man, the banker and his wife, the banker Nelson.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know, there's quite a lot of them kind of stuck up a little bit. They lived up in that end of town. We were just coming folks and we'd all have a lot more fun than they did. But they wouldn't get together with you down here. There wasn't that kind of mixing. No, they didn't. They didn't mix with us. They were just. And then later on they had what they called a, well, the 500 Club. They used to play 500. And then it got to be kind of a whole pulley one way or another. The ones that liked to play bridge or twist a lot. They kind of had a club of their own. And they didn't want to associate but they had, what was it, they called a, they called the other bunch, oh, some funny name, just a kind of hick name. Hay seeds. Well, something like that, you know. But I can't think of any of it. Anyhow, it finally got divided. The group bunch and the other group, they didn't like the, the lower bunch didn't like the other bunch really as well. And the others were, it was just mutual. The other group didn't like the razzabatazz kids or whatever they called somebody. I can't think of the name now but anyway, we didn't let them bother. They met some place after they got the meeting out, you know, playing cards or something. That was in later years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had separate meeting nights and you're on your own, right?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But some of them felt a little bit slighted because they could play bridge, you know, and play some of what the others played. And they weren't asked to join their club. They thought they were just a little bit snooty. But most of us didn't care. They didn't care. They had enough of their own in their own group. Just a little bit, when it came to the party, so the gatherings were just kind of a little bit aside from things of their own. But now when it came to mingling with them through town or something's going on in town for everybody's interested in, nobody was very sociable. Were the card clubs mostly, were they mostly for people who were the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Bontons? Mostly for them?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, it was just some of them decided they could have a little club of their own because they liked to play bridge. Well, bridge wasn't as common then as a pinnacle or something like that to them. Most of them could play some kind of card game or... They were a little bit... they common did, but they didn't go so much for 500 or... No, no, I mean... 500 or West or...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that other one I named?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Bridge. Now something like that was their game. I see. Whereas the more... the ordinary folks played... were likely to play Pinochle and that kind of thing. Yeah, and hearts and, oh, you know, any kind of games, just common games. I don't think I played any of them because I didn't know how.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the people who were on Bug Row, do they call it Bug Row, by the way?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They're kind of fond times. There's a Muck -E -Mucks or High Tones or something. It was something that, just speaking of it, sometimes they wanted to be a little bit nasty about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did people know that these folks thought of themselves as being Muck -E -Mucks?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I guess they didn't bother much. They thought that they were... They wanted to be... pay no attention to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What I mean is, how did they know that these upper crust people thought they were upper crust in those days?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well... I mean, these days it would be you'd have a Cadillac or you'd have a fancy house. Well, they probably had a big house. They had mostly large houses in there and pretty nice furniture and pretty well fixed stuff. And the rest of them, well, they were very common, but most of them had things real comfortable and ordinary.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do these well -fixed people actually talk down to people a little bit?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, I don't seem to mean like they did. Sometimes I suppose they made remarks about the Rinky -Dinks. That's who they were when they got their car closed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Rinky -Dinks were the people who were the common people?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So they got in the 500s, the Rinky -Dinks. Well, but you say they... I just wonder how somebody who thinks he's superior shows it to somebody who he thinks he's better than. Well, they kind of splurged, they kind of dressed, kind of foxy, you know, kind of elaborate. And the Rinky -Dinks kind of thought it's kind of foolish just driving around dressed up in birdcages, but I guess that was their way of doing it. And then I guess they just could have forgotten they stayed within their reach, each one kind of minding their own business. But yet they'd all go to some kind of something that's going on for everybody, and they were nice neighbors, you know, but it's just the social clubs and all. They kind of made a separation. Now, Mrs. Ellison was in the social group, and Mrs. Nelson, and all, but they were just the nicest people here. So I just could meet them on the street, you know. I know they were always often nice to meet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they mostly management people and the business people of the town? Is that who they were?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: There was quite a few, yes, that way. It was the bankers, I said, and then the doctor's wife, and the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The logging bosses?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, Mrs. Jones, and some of the kind of more upper -divine towns. That way, more than... But I don't think any of them really snubbed them exactly, the other. Just doing things like that, we just kind of each one took his own path and thought nothing of it. But when something unusual was going on, and everybody's business, you know, they all mangled, and there's always a meeting on the street, but they'd stop and talk to you, and they'd talk in chats, you know. They were just all nice common people. That card party kind of got...tried to make a split between them, but it didn't bother them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were those the people who were more likely to be interested in politics and that kind of thing, too?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I wonder if they were the ones who were the politicians and were trying to get into... I don't think any of them here were really running for any offices or anything like that. And I don't know, I never paid much attention to it because I didn't care much about politics, but I can't remember that they ever let politics get too deep into it. Now then, we were speaking about Mrs. Cryer and Mrs. Murphy and some of those. Now, they were in that group. They, oh, I don't know, later might have been in the other group, but they came from the east, Minnesota, I think it was. So they were in the group with our parties that we had.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know, I guess the other folks invited those people to all their nice parties, you know, because their fathers or their husbands or somebody were connected with them. They were in the potlatch jobs, that is, the better jobs or the boss jobs or something like that. I think Mrs. Fisher was probably in that group, too, some more. Well, she was later. When she started teaching, she taught here for, well, she's been teaching for, I think, my finger, 20 years. And she was taught all the way from the primary corruptive high school. First one was Braden and then another. And today somebody was just saying, well, if your kid goes to school with Marie Fisher, he'll either know something or else he can't get it. She was pounding into him. She didn't know something when she gets through it. She was. She had a really good reputation as a teacher. Yes, she sat right down on him and she didn't get something. You had to have an awful good reason why you didn't.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, I wanted to ask you about, I heard that there's a story that goes along with when you and Ernie got married and went on your honeymoon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I've never heard anything about that. I think that was all in that book that the trees grew tall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you ever had that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, I've read that. I don't remember it in there. I don't know if he was in it or not, but he got everything like that he could. But we were going to be married. I don't know who started that, whether Ernie had suggested it or whether it was one way. His brother's wife's sister lived in St. Mary's and they called her Aunt Sadie. Her name was Sadie Fielding. They lived up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It might be that she said, hey, when are you going to get married or something?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, coming up to my place and going to my house or something like that. I don't know. Something like that might have been the trouble starter of it. Or maybe he just might have said, well, that's it. We have to go up to see you. I don't know how that... But anyway, it was arranged that we would go to St. Mary's. We married today at Sadie's. So, we went down and caught the train down here at, I think at four o 'clock in the afternoon. It was a passenger train.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It was kind of a dull date. I don't remember if I had to go in cloudy or something. But anyhow, we caught the train at four and his brother and his wife and the little girl, she was three, went with us.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We started out and went up the railroad to St. Mary's. When we got up to Clarkia or Fernwood, we were at Clarkia. That's only about 12 -15 miles from here. We got up there, we were stopped, and there was a log train off the track. The logger had gone up ahead of us, spilled up logs and tore up the track. And there we sat.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So, all we could do was just sit there and wait until it got fixed. We sat there for a couple hours or more. I don't know how long they'd been working on that wreck before we ever went up there and knew anything about it. When we got there, that's what we found. Louise from home. We waited and waited and finally things patched up so they let us by. We were supposed to be married at eight o 'clock. But that time it was pouring rain, just to pour it down. And St. D 'Nuckleville had been down to the depot to meet us at eight o 'clock and weren't there. And they said it would be delayed quite a while. They told them that the depot then had been delayed. Well, they came back after a while. A few minutes or half hours. They came back to that raid and met us again and they worked there. So, we didn't get in there until about ten o 'clock or something like that. It was pouring down rain and when they got there, the minister had been waiting at her place for quite a while then to marry us. And anyhow, he said, well I'll just have to go. I can't wait any longer because he promised to marry another couple up in the other end of town. And it was time for their appointment then. And he said, well tell the folks if they want to wait until this performance is over, I'll be back. And so we waited around after we got down to their place and we had to wait a while until he got his performance done for the other couple. And then he came back to us. And so that was, I don't know, there was somebody else.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And well I just found this again after this thing was lost and I was just having fits about it because I was so proud of it. I kept it so good. And then it finally came to light. And I don't know what that is. Something I've wanted to keep I guess. Yes, there's our picture. A lovely wedding picture.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Why did you decide on St.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mary's instead of Bovill?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well I don't know. Well there wasn't any place that I really wanted to see. Oh that's a play that we were in in later years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A play in Bovill?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh yes, we had a home talent play here every once in a while. A bunch of us that come together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What play was this?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh we had several of them. The Great Winderson Mine and Biscuits and Bills. I don't know what the names of them were. This is Marvin. This is the one that she was married to. This is the second generation. See after Marvin had grown up. And the Barnacles. We had Barnacles bills they were. And I was placed up in a balcony. It's supposed to be. I fixed a balcony. And Leo Guilfoy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you know?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I sure did know him. He's one of the old timers and he's full of dibble.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And he sang the other part. He was singing to me. And I said, I'll come down and let you in. I'll come down and let you in. And I don't know how it all went but anyhow that was that part of it. Oh it all turned out funny in the long run. And then another play was The Great Winderson Mine. I was supposed to be a little schoolgirl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Great what?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: The Winderson Mine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Winderson Mine?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I was a little schoolgirl just staying with this old farmer and his family and going to school and working for La Vonde. And then Ernie's nephew Bill Smith was. And he was a great pal of mine. And he was sweet as could be. He's passed away now. But he said that he was supposed to be a schoolboy that was working for his board. They're doing the chores and all that. And going to school there. And of course he had a crush on me.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And there's another fellow that was John McGeary. I don't know whether you've heard of McGeary's mute when I was him. And he was supposed to be the city slicker. He came along and he of course was making a play for me to get other information about this old farmer's place. And I'd heard that he was mine on that. And so he was being nice to me. He'd get all the information he could. And that made Bill mad. He didn't like him at all. He'd do everything he could every time he'd see me talking to him. And so finally one time he came in just in time and Bill went and planned it in his play. And he went and opened the lid to a box or something. And he says, sick of it. And his dog jumped out there and ran off, chased him just across the stage. And that old invention boy had been saying that one of the other fellows, Hollenbeck, he was wacky over inventions. And so he said he invented a good invention. He said, you just lift up the lid and chuck in a hunk of beef or pork and turn the crank and out comes all the rest of the milling. And that spiel went with every time he'd seen a buddy. That same spiel went out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a real dog that jumped out? Did the dog chase him? The dog was trained to do that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes. And Bill got his dog there, you know, and he knew. He said, sick of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it Bill's dog?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And he chased John and put that off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was in the play?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah. So, anyway, we used to have real good plays. We had the old man's convention here. And, well, that was the regular riot. They offered, everybody could see it as big crowd, and they offered, I don't know, double or something dollar ticket anyhow. They offered us extra if we put it on again the next day or two.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, you did?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't think we put it on again that day. We put on another one later on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what was the old maid's convention about?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: That sounds like a pretty good one. Well, it was just all women there, and each they were having a big convention, and all these old maids came, and one of them was a woman, she was about eight feet tall, more or less. She was an awful tall woman. And they called her, her name was Tiny Short. And they know all of them with crazy names like that. And then I was Rebecca Stout, I think it was. And they were all there to work out ways and plans and means of getting a husband. They were all working to get a husband. And one had a way and idea, and then another one. And they were trying to decide what all to do about it. And then each one had to make their speech about what they thought would be the best way.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And finally, I think that was another play, I guess. But anyhow, one play was either in that one or in another one. And these girls or ladies were supposed to be made over because they just didn't seem to be very attractive or something. So they were going to put them, he had a big invention, and so he put them into this cabinet. And he asked them all their ages and everything like that, but he cautioned them they must tell the truth. Sometimes it was disastrous. So he put this one in there, and then they had to turn the crank for a little while, a few times. And then she'd come out to whatever this wonderful girl that she wanted to be, you know, all dressed up. And it was just another person that was ready and dressed for that. And then one of them, she wanted to be, he'd ask what she wanted to be when they were in there. So she wanted to be twins. And so they got them all fixed in, and then she turned this machine on and on. And these two little short girls, you know, all frilly little dressed, tip toeing around, the twins. And another one, one woman, she just couldn't resist the light about her age. She wouldn't have been able to tell that. And so they put her in the machine and turned, and pretty soon all the tin cans and glass and stuff you remember. So I became rattling down in the thing, and I'd forgotten what she turned out to be or how the rest of it. And she got all this awful clatter and awful noise. She didn't tell the truth about her age. Oh, I see. This wasn't the old maid's convention. This was another play. I don't remember. It's been so long since I, I don't remember how much we've put on so many of them. I don't remember just which one was which, whether they... Whether it was part of the same play or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what your favorite part was that you ever played?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, yes, I remember most of those. When I was, well, when I was a little girl going to school, I made me a wig of rope, unraveled it all out, and fixed the, dyed it, black dye. And then I did great long braids on each side, heavy braids, tied to the big red ribbon. And I was dressed, oh, just like a schoolgirl. So, and then the one time the old maid's convention, we headed up to church, and I don't know how that was the same as the part of the replay of the one we had down at the hall, or whether it was. But anyhow, I think it was a different one that we had. And I was dressed up as a very snippy, high -toned lady, and I was dressed in my feather hat, big velvet hat, and a big feather in it, and I don't know all the trinkets. And someplace, they got that picture, I think Marie Fisher has it, and I was coming down the back steps up there at the church, and they got my picture just sucking down the steps, they were taking pictures of the different ones. But I was kind of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who usually put these plays together? How did they ever get going?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, it was Bill Smith, mostly. It was Bill and his uncle, Bill and Purd.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Purd Hughes? Yeah, you know him too, do you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, I know of him. Well, that was his uncle, and his sister Mabel, that was my chum. And, uh... What about the... I heard that the Parker boy was quite interesting. Oh, really Parker. He was professional, and he traveled all through the East, the Orient, and all, and he was quite a part of the troop. And he was, well, professional, he was speaking, he was just real good. He was, well, he took his training, or he studied in Moscow at the University of Idaho there for a while. But anyhow, when he got through, he signed up with this company, and they were glad to get him. He was just real, real good. But the trouble with it, he drank. And in the long run, I guess he met his Waterloo and Spokane down on Skid Row. He was awfully nice. He was smart as a whip, but just couldn't let liquor alone. I heard that he, during the winters sometimes, he'd come back here to rest and recuperate, and he'd get involved in the plays here. That's true. And I won the play, the old couple of plays, I know that I saw that when he came back. But, I want to be, now that I've eventually named him, and Mrs. Well, one of the, Mrs. Harkins lived over on that street near us, in the next door to us. And she said, and they took, one of them was, anyhow, she took the part, and she was down in the lady. She must have been fifty or more. And she was kind of dressed like an old rey. And she was always talking about her patent medicine. And she said, everybody she talked to, she always had something to say about her patent medicine. And, they were very good plays that we had picked. And one of them, I was, Bill and I alone put on a skit in between changes of scenery. And so, there was just three months difference in Bill's age and mine. We were just sitting right behind the other school. Not as much as it did for brother. And anyhow, he was supposed to be this young married couple that we were. And he was the man, I was the wife. And how she came, I didn't know anything. I was just greeting them. So, he called, I think, over the phone. And I don't know how they were telling him what all to have for dinner or something. And so, I got out the recipe book and, well, you had two eggs and I was breaking them into the container. And then you had, I just put in the two eggs, I think, and didn't break them. I think just dumped them in, break themselves. And I had, oh I don't know, whatever it was, was, oh, and salt. I think it said, it didn't say how much, so it just dumped in a cup full of salt. And that's the recipe for it. Oh, nobody could have eaten it, but I didn't know how to cook, but I was trying to fix him a nice dinner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He talked to you over the phone?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, he had to come over the phone or something. Anyhow, I promised to fix him a nice dinner. I was going to surprise him and fix him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he giving you instructions on how to cook on the phone or were you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, they had, someone had told me to do it and I had a cookbook there to look into it. And it wasn't, whatever it said, it made plenty of allowance, you know, for people that knew how to cook. And so, I didn't suppose anything. And I was going to bake tea cakes for supper, dinner. And so it said, well, it didn't say how much tea, so I just dumped in a bunch of tea and all, I don't know, all kinds of crazy things like that. And then finally I went to mixing it with my hands and I had to open both hands and I didn't know how to get it off. And I started to cry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Get it all over your face?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes. And then he came home and I was just in tears and awful fixed and finally we decided to go out to dinner. And she called her dad to tell her she just wanted to come home to mother again. And on the long run, my dad sent us a check and patched things all up. We got started again. It was just a little quick thing, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, had you made up the skit?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, just a little short play. Just two people, you know, just wanted to pick. Just get some pills.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you said Leo was real active in those plays, huh?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Leo was real active in those plays.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's quite a funny fellow, wasn't he?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh -huh. He was just full of life and pep all the time and he's always had something to say.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't remember all the things just now that I... Well, you remember quite a few. One time I was the dressmaker. I was supposed to be the dressmaker of the town and I don't know, kind of a leading part.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the plays, do you think, give people, give a lot of people a chance to really perform and get up on stage and entertain the town?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, there didn't so many. I mean, just our bunch, we got started. And our bunch would get together with another play and start. I could tell one story that I don't like. I won't put it on here, though. Maybe you could tell it without using the names. If it's a good story about how things were. No, it isn't. It's very good, but not as a sin. Everybody didn't know that.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I guess I would better leave that sometime. Here, just tell them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you and newlyweds in those days, when you were young, when they first got married, did most of them have a lot of learning to do?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And how to get along with each other and how to make a go of life?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I think they were more settled a little bit. Some of them get along all right now. And yet, some of them, I've had some of that training in school. Some of these home acts and all those things. Now, they've taken sewing at school, they've taken cooking, and they've got quite a little start. But what we had at that time, Mother taught us. We knew how to wash dishes and how to cook spuds and things. And we knew all those things. Mother leaves them for us to do sometimes. She says, you make a salad, now do this. We just learned as we grew up. But I don't know. I don't know. I don't think that they were so...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They were so much different in some ways. There's a bunch that are the same and there's a bunch that are different. At both times, it seemed like part of them are... that do know how to get along. I was just raised in a common family and we had work to do. Most of the people, especially if they had large families, they generally give the kids a job. They're supposed to do it. And they generally saw it as what they did.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But they... I don't know the kids. Well, in those days now, in the beginning in those days when we used to have those parties and all. Well, now today you talk about having a popcorn party or something. No. Kids aren't interested to know it all. They want a car. They want to go turn around the car. They like a little snorty tooth. They want to spike some punch or something. They've got to do different than that. But we didn't have any of that. Sometimes, it would have... season permitted. We'd have cider. But we used to have popcorn, you know. Pop up a whole dishpan full of popcorn and have that. And maybe popcorn bowls made. And maybe another time, we'd have a big dishpan full of apples.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And then, I remember one particular night, we had taffy pull. And some of them didn't know anything about it. They were pulling taffy and they'd get down and go with a big chunk of it on the floor. And they'd travel. It was such a mess. But they all had a lot of fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the family in those days was more stable when you were getting married? Were husband and wife really married? Were they closer and more likely to stay together?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Or does that make...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: is that a good question?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I don't know. It seems like that you didn't hear of so many divorces. Of course, there's more people now, too. But you didn't hear of so many divorces. You'd get married now and then maybe three weeks, you'd get a divorce. Well, I think about how you and Ernie knew each other for years and years before you got married. And I would think that you'd get to know each other pretty well that way before you ever did get married. Yes, and I knew he had lots of faults. And I suppose I knew that I had some, too, probably. You usually can't see your own faults as well as you can the other fellas. But anyhow, and to tell the long and short of it was when we were going together there, you know, a long while. We didn't own each other's habits and ways quite a lot. But anyhow, when we did get married, his folks now, by that time, his sister had moved here from south Missouri. She had a large family and then there was his brother and his family from Missouri. And they got to be quite a bunch of his relatives down here. So when we got married, they must sort of say, oh, just wait. It'll be just six months. And that's as long as they last. Give them six months to live and be all over with. And I said, I'd like to see some of them back now again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think they thought you were going to last?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, because he had such a rotten temper and disposition. And he was so fussy and he'd been humored to death.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Humored what?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Humored. Spoiled. Spoiled on him. And most of them kind of catered to him rather than to have him a job. I was cool and calm and took things in stride. I tried to get along. But I spent the first year or so of my marriage trying half the time. But then, like I said, going along helped. Anyhow, I was easily hurt. I am yet. And so whenever anything would go wrong, I'd spend time crying. I got over it. But anyhow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would Ernie do when you cried?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He'd gone to work probably at that time. And he wasn't the one that's ever known to after he'd say a lot of dirty mean stuff or mean and onward kind of stuff. He wasn't the guy that comes and apologizes. He just wasn't in his book. An apology would have made a wonderful lot of difference to me. It would have just kind of patched all that up in a little while. He'd at least come to me and said he's sorry or he'd made a mistake or something. But nope. He didn't do that. But you knew when you married him that this was the kind of guy he was. Yeah, I know. Of course, I thought I'd make him over, like a lot of the girls do.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But I don't know. Maybe I needed some making over too. I couldn't see it. But anyhow, that's what the folks said. They just gave him six months because he knew what disposition he had. And kind of overbearing, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember, it hasn't been so long since I got married myself, but I'm wondering, can you remember in those days what the kinds of things would be that would upset a wife?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know. Some of them still hang on, I guess. But he used to make a remark about cooking. Maybe he didn't mean. About something I know. The first few weeks, I guess, after I was married or the first month or so, he said, we should fix lemon pie some of these days. I just love lemon pie. And I said, I could eat a dozen lemon pies. So I said, well, I'll try some these days. And I thought, well, I'll take him by surprise. So I spent one day after he went to work, I started in to make my lemon pies. And so I got comfortable with the recipe and made them. And all of them looked so pretty and I made six of them to be sure I had enough for two of us. That's what those things for his wife knows how to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The wife knows what?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Knows how to do or how not to do. I made six pies. And he said he made the remark that I could eat a dozen pies right now. And so I made those and I had them for supper. He tasted the lemon pie and I don't know what the remark was. He said, good, brief.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the matter with this?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, what's the matter now?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I thought you liked lemon pies. I said, well, I can't eat them things. And he started to tell me it was a bit nice. He said it just let loose both hands. And so, of course, that hurt my feelings. God worked so hard to get those nice pies fixed for him. And then he should say something like that. I thought that was terrible. And so I tasted the pie and I found out it wasn't a matter. I couldn't eat it either.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I knew something had gone wrong. Still, he didn't have to talk like that. He would have been nice about it. He didn't have to talk like that. So those little things sometimes make an awful difference. And sometimes it's just the way you say them. But I found out it was bitter as quinine. And the recipe called for so much lemons and so much eggs and so forth. And you put in the juice of so many lemons and the grated rind of the lemon ocean flavor. Well, I did all that. But I grated it down into the white, you know, too. And that made it just bitter, spiny. I just barely grated off the oil, you know, on the outside. I found that out later. But I said I never would make him another one. I had just long time after that after I told the neighbor what had happened. And she told me that's what doesn't matter. I made one once in a while, but I made it while he was going to work and he didn't see it. If it didn't turn out just right, I ate part of it and put the rest in the garbage. So I forgot if I could turn out a pie that wouldn't taste like it was supposed to. Well, when...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what you do when you get upset?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: You just cry and just... You know, let it go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you wouldn't just get mad at him back? You'd cry instead of getting mad?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes. I would think when you were first married there would be a lot of things that could come up like that because when you're living with someone for... There are lots of adjustments to be made on both sides, usually.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And then later, after I became pregnant, he bought chicken for dinner. I was supposed to have chicken ready when he came home that night. And so, I think it was on Sunday. He was going to have to work that... Get some work Sunday. So he always would chop the chicken's head off and pick it for me. Then I could finish. And so, this time he went away and he forgot to kill the chicken. So... And he gave me strict orders, be sure and have chicken and dumplings. And so, I went to get some chicken and I got home. Well, that was all right. That was fine. I liked it too. But when I went to get it, there was a chicken in a box. I think it was from somebody who had it under a box and it was alive.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I thought, oh dear, now what am I going to do?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I had never cut the chicken's head off in my life. And I didn't think I could do it. And I was pregnant. And I don't know, it must have been four or five months. And anyhow, he... I was about to scream. I was nervous. So I put it up there again and whack again and I'd get it on the back of its neck. And duck, you know, first one and then I couldn't say anything. And I thought, I've got to do it now. I can't let the poor thing run loose and suffer. And I thought, I've got to cut it off. Oh, I guess I could do most. And so I finally, four or five times, I tried and I finally got its head off. And then it laid out there, that head laid out there and blinked its eyes at me. I thought, I croak.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever see one?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I was just a fifth after didn't know that. And I thought that I'll buy on my baby. I thought that sure, I'll mark my baby. That they had that horror of that thing's eyes blinking at me. And I think I started crying about that. Got my boots. I had to put in a butcher's old hand a little bit of time. But I finally got it cleaned up and he came back. I wasn't in very good mood. And I came home and I said, the next time you want a chicken, you cook it. I said, you better be mighty sure it's already killed. Because I was suffering everything that day trying to get that chicken dinner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were married in those first years, was it a matter of order you around and tell you what to do? Was it like that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, kind of. It didn't do him a whole lot of good though. I was easy going and had to cooperate on everything. But I like to reason. They can take things for a reason. He doesn't even have much of a hand to reason about things.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: The in -laws too. I would think having a lot of his relatives around would have some effect too. Well, I was just going to tell you about going to a vet like that. A little article came out that was supposed to be told. It was a very sluggish remark. It was about me. So I was making a play for one of his nephews. Well, that boy came here all the time. He considered me more than he wanted to be. He'd come to me instead of his mother. He was an awfully nice kid. He'd come to me and say, well, you think about this and so and so. He asked about the girl he was thinking about marrying. Maybe you knew Butler's down on the ridge. The name doesn't... Well, anyhow, her name was Butler. He was asking what I thought of her and what I thought about his getting married. I told him. I said, well, it's not for me to say. That's your doings, but all I can tell you is what I do or what I think. I said, I know she's an awfully nice girl and I think she'd make a wonderful wife. There's other things to think of. I said, you'll have debts and bills to pay and all that. I think I had for that. I said, I don't think you can find anybody to be any better. I said, I recommend you. He said, not an awfully nice girl. I said, that's a good kind to have. He was getting it all in and we talked about it a long time. He used to come down to me. He'd probably meet heavy and come down and ask me about what I thought about. Well, I wasn't so terribly much older than he was, but anyhow, he just took my advice on a lot of things. This is where I got out at one of the parties and a big family bunch had told a nice story on me. I was chasing him. I had a crush on him. It kind of reverted to slander in the long run. I knew that he came here and visited us all the time when he wanted to. When that story got back, I found out what I said that hurts my feelings. When he got home from work, I told him I wanted him to come and eat and suffer. I said, I've got a little matter to settle. He went to my father's house and I said, well, so and so told this big long story on me and I said, I'm not standing for it. I said, it's degrading to him and it's also to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, you're not going to tell stuff like that, are you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He wasn't going to tell me. The first thing the family told me, one thing he said, he said, you just got a bunch of hell in your tonight. That's what's the matter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, well, that's how you feel, is it?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I kind of begged him and told him what it's all about. He knew it wasn't so and so did I. He knew himself it wasn't so. After I talked long enough, I said, all right, brother, you stay here. You stay here. I'm going. I'm out there fighting my own battles. If you don't want to fight for me, I'll fight my own battles. I said, now, look, the whole bunch will have to. I said, well, when I got ready to go, I said, well, I'll go along. I said, I'll go with you. I said, well, don't bother you any. I said, don't make it. I said, well, I'll go. I'll go. I said, well, I'll go. You're going to go, and you wouldn't do it. So I said, I don't care where you go or not. Well, he was muscled right along and went right along with me. He didn't say anything. I said, you don't have to. I'm not asking you to take part in anything, but I said, you tell what you know and what you don't know. So he went with me. At that particular time, there was a whole bunch of the group together. The ones that had been, a couple of them that had been supposed to have made this talk were right there with the rest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so finally I said to one of the parties, I said, so what did you think if I told you I heard something other than I would have had to really something about somebody that would be the same predicament that was trying to put me in?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And he said, no, no. Well, I just wouldn't pay much attention to it in the overalls. I had that happen to me. One time, years ago, somebody told something to me.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I said, no. I didn't talk about the subject. I said, well, Bill, I didn't know that. That was just a story, a similar story that I was fixed up to see how you would feel in my position.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And he had settled his opinion. And then I told him what this other was. And I said, I'm not just about to let it slip that way either. I said, no, the parties are right here. They're supposed to have started the thing. And I said, it's up to them. And I said, and one of them, the only person who got me irate and nervous, pidgety, she was just in a chair. And she just started laying on top of me. I said, just grabbed her with her shoulders and walked up behind the chair and grabbed her with her shoulders. And I said, now you sit still here until you hear the rest of this. I said, you're going to hear this, all of it. And I said, and the rest of them. I said, I don't intend for things like that to be told about me. And so, she just calmed down. She hadn't held her head down there. She hadn't listened. And anyhow, finally, while they all denied it, they hadn't anybody said a thing. And that they, it was all, why they wouldn't have thought of such a thing. I said, how much you know what I heard, that that's what, it came from here. And so, I said, and I didn't know about to live under that. So, but they were all just a nice aspire. They all made all sorts of apologies, you know, that they didn't want anything to do with it. They didn't, or wouldn't think of it, of any accusations or anything like that. So, anyhow, I talked to them, but I kind of got things settled. And then, my mind got them all big and my partner and all this and that. And so, I figured that everybody was settled and everybody denied it. And I said, well, I hadn't heard it. And so, I went down and I wanted to speak up, that they were so sure of themselves. But they finally, they all fished out of it and they declared they didn't have anything to do with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, well, now, if you're sure it's going to stay that way, why go forget it then?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Because I said, I don't intend to live under that. And so, I left them, came out home and I never really heard any more about it. They're my best of friends. I always have been the best of friends all through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, had one of them told you about it in the first place? How did you ever get the news?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't remember how I got the news. I came through somebody, rather. It sounds like that your doing that was a lot better than if you hadn't done it, it just would have kept going. Yes. And it could have been a nasty mess. But I had the fighting clothes on and it wasn't very good. Unreal for me. I never did any scrapping. But I drew at them on that night. And I bought it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: When I was staying home from school, I found a way in the middle. I just threw myself across the street and started crying and praying.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I'll get tuned up here pretty soon. And that's when I told them, you just stay here. Do as you please and I'll do the same. And I thought, I'll thresh it out right now, but it's hot. And so, everybody wouldn't confess anything. And I said, it's not going to make any trouble to other people.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I said, I don't want them confessing over me. There was nothing to it. They were good friends, too. They were the best friends. When I got through, I told them all about it and thought about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean this guy and his girl?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes. And they were just newly married then. That's when I finally got me rung into that. But anyhow, they didn't stay that way. I found them all there. It's funny.
